posts.cooked:
As someone who writes about wine, I try to keep aware of what is going on in the industry around me. Because I put a lot of effort into the layout and photography that appears in my digital and museum-quality print work, I’m especially interested in what publications do for imagery, and more specifically, if they produce a hard copy, what does it look like?

Back in 2017, TWA made an announcement of a new look for their familiar perfect-bound, tan hard copy. Issue 231 was obviously something new with a glossy cover and white paper. So I bought a copy just to look over. Apart from the curious lack of margins (and only nine ‘small’ snapshots in 190+ pages) it still contained the reliable content TWA subscribers had come to expect. So, here are my questions:

Are they still producing a hard copy? If so, what does it look like?
If not, when did they stop? Was 231 a ‘one-time thing? I don’t see an option listed on their website.
If you know of any other influential, image-rich (advertising-free) wine review publications that do both digital and hard copy, please let me know. (I know about Connoisseurs’ Guide started by my friend, Charlie Olken, which pretty much hasn’t changed in decades)

Doing print is an expensive endeavor and anyone doing less than 500 copies while maintaining museum-quality production values is probably an artist, and losing money.

November and December are busy months for tasting and photography and January is usually when I sit down, look at failures and successes of the previous year, do a competitive analysis and decide how I may offer more value in my work going forward. Premium Content and concept will be the two main things I examine (in addition to scrubbing the list).

Thanks in advance.

posts.name: Philip N. Jones
posts.cooked: No, they don’t do a print edition. I’m guessing it stopped about there years ago? More than that? I had a print subscription, but they never even sent me a renewal notice for the electronic edition. They simply stopped coming.

posts.name: Philip N. Jones
posts.cooked: Time flies. Apparently it happened in 2013.

Time flies. Apparently it happened in 2013.

Thanks, Philip. I do have tangible proof they had a new look as of #231 in June 2017. My question was about anything after that and what the status is in present day.

posts.name: Philip N. Jones
posts.cooked: Maybe someone can give us a definitive answer. I threw away all of the issues I had been saving.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The last print edition was June 2019. By that point, it won’t surprise you to learn that the vast majority of our subscribers had migrated to online, so the expense and vast time commitment no longer made sense.

posts.name: Andrew Demaree
posts.cooked: Seems worth adding that some of those subscribers probably gravitated to online when it was announced that the paper edition would no longer contain all of the reviews that were going to be posted for online subscribers. That was certainly the case for me.

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
Issues #231 June 2017, #232 Aug, #233 October, were full color/glossy as you describe, then went back to their traditional appearance. (Or at least 231-3 were the only glossy editions I retained).
Like William mentioned, during summer 2019 I got notified the publication was migrating to full online and invited to follow. Never got around to re-upping.

The last print edition was June 2019. By that point, it won’t surprise you to learn that the vast majority of our subscribers had migrated to online, so the expense and vast time commitment no longer made sense.

Thanks, William. Yes, it is expensive to produce a hardcopy version of anything. I committed to doing both digital, and high-quality print from the beginning 10 years ago since I knew I would not be competitive on a scale with the likes of TWA - I needed to do something differently.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The last print edition was June 2019. By that point, it won’t surprise you to learn that the vast majority of our subscribers had migrated to online, so the expense and vast time commitment no longer made sense.

Thanks, William. Yes, it is expensive to produce a hardcopy version of anything. I committed to doing both digital, and high-quality print from the beginning 10 years ago since I knew I would not be competitive on a scale with the likes of TWA - I needed to do something differently.

Aside from the expense, the other big issue was the need to have a long lead-time for print articles, meaning two deadlines for every report (print and online). This caused a huge amount of additional work for reviewers and also everyone behind the scenes.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
The last print edition was June 2019. By that point, it won’t surprise you to learn that the vast majority of our subscribers had migrated to online, so the expense and vast time commitment no longer made sense.

I have fond memories of people bringing in their print copies. Like they were suddenly going to find something that we didn’t know about.

We generally had all of the new reviews up within a day, even though we had to hand type them out (since you couldn’t copy and paste).

It was a race to buy too, which was also half the fun.

The last print edition was June 2019. By that point, it won’t surprise you to learn that the vast majority of our subscribers had migrated to online, so the expense and vast time commitment no longer made sense.

It was a race to buy too, which was also half the fun.

I also remember people walking into my store with their wine publications festooned with yellow stickies only to learn it was already in our client cellars. “Would you like to receive my monthly wine email?”

posts.name: Charles Fuss
posts.cooked:
I went to my wa subscription page to update it to get a print copy. I was disappointed to read it doesn’t exist. I don’t think it needs to be color, but I feel people will buy it if it is available


I’ve never subscribed to decanter and I didn’t like ws, with all of its advertising and fluff… for print , who makes the best publication?

posts.name: Monica Larner
posts.cooked:
Hi Doug,

We don’t miss those massive print edition deadlines, and I would argue that we produce more/better content without them.

You asked about photography. At the Wine Advocate, each reviewer is responsible for supplying the images for the articles he/she writes. Some of us like taking photos more than others and this is reflected in what we publish.

When we revamped the print edition years ago, the plan was to include more photography. In reality, we always had more notes and text than any issue could house. Our editors were forced to make big cuts to article length and the number of wine notes included. Considering the physical constraints of the print edition, photography was always first to be cut out.

Best,
Monica

posts.name: Charles Fuss
posts.cooked: I liked the crappy old paper bag looking print … pics are nice but not having a physical rag is worse

posts.name: Monica Larner
posts.cooked:
You’re not alone Charles, but I suspect you are in the minority.
Not having a print edition has been a win-win-win.

  1. The reader gets more content and timely coverage (we publish weekly now instead of every two months).
  2. The reviewer has more flexibility and greater creative outreach (adding more photos, videos, longer intro articles and detailed producer notes).
  3. The planet gets less paper waste.

Best,
Monica

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked: With the move to more frequent “issues” of the “magazine” online, yesterday’s “February 2022 Week 1” issue sets (or at least ties - I let my subscription lapse there for a while) the new record for smallest TWA issue ever!

posts.cooked: The problem with the WA online edition is that it is poorly designed and difficult to navigate. Both Vinous and Dunnuck are better.

posts.name: R Davis
posts.cooked: I subscribed again after a many year hiatus, largely because of Mr. Kelley’s contributions here which sparked a deeper interest in his work and regions that I’m not very knowledgeable in (all roads lead to Burgundy, right?). While I knew the print version was dropped some time ago, in the back of my mind, I expected a PDF version (Dunnuck does this and it is great) so i could read a set of regional reviews in one cohesive body. However, the presentation now is raw data that one must search or click through is very cumbersome unless I am want to look up a specific wine. I would rather read (or scan) a cohesive report without having to be distracted by a bunch of mouse clicks. If a PDF type format becomes available, I’ll likely continue the subscription.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Despite all the negative comments, I find that I have little issue locating the information I want on the Wine Advocate site. Maybe I am just more focused in what I am looking for, but a simple search gets the job done.

That said, I am rarely looking for some broad regional overview, as I have reached a point where I know what I like.

posts.name: Charles Fuss
posts.cooked:
I feel people will buy it if it is available

Sadly, this was not the case!

Yeah, but i think now everyone has gone digital, it is too crowded. The pendulum swung too far. Maybe try just “bordeaux” and “napa” in basic prints (in the same old style that workd for 30+ years), make it a subscription buy up and put that in the mail before releasing the online scores/notes.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
I feel people will buy it if it is available

Sadly, this was not the case!

Yeah, but i think now everyone has gone digital, it is too crowded. The pendulum swung too far. Maybe try just “bordeaux” and “napa” in basic prints (in the same old style that workd for 30+ years), make it a subscription buy up and put that in the mail before releasing the online scores/notes.

And with current conditions you will receive your hard copy two years after the online scores are posted…if you ever get it at all.

Hi Doug,

We don’t miss those massive print edition deadlines, and I would argue that we produce more/better content without them.

You asked about photography. At the Wine Advocate, each reviewer is responsible for supplying the images for the articles he/she writes. Some of us like taking photos more than others and this is reflected in what we publish.

When we revamped the print edition years ago, the plan was to include more photography. In reality, we always had more notes and text than any issue could house. Our editors were forced to make big cuts to article length and the number of wine notes included. Considering the physical constraints of the print edition, photography was always first to be cut out.

Best,
Monica

Thanks, Monica. My experience as a wine critic has been different. There isn’t any difference in the content between digital and print editions, and demand for hard copy shows positive growth over the last 4 years (which coincides with shifting to shooting studio-quality portraits of winemakers with their reviews). My only ‘physical constraint’ for print is when it exceeds the 1st class mail weight limit (about 84 pages).

Doug - you also set yourself some parameters that didn’t exist for the WA or many other journals. I suppose that if the WA only focused on say, Bordeaux, and had a critic nearby, it would be easier. Your way is understandable for your work (I don’t know how lucrative it is though!) but with multiple regions and multiple reviewers and a bit more administrative overhead, I get why the WA shifted. I’m surprised that other magazines haven’t shifted over too - WS, WE, Food and Wine, etc. Maybe the subscriber list is easier to verify than eyeballs for advertisers, but I have a stack of things I haven’t even looked at.

Never thought it would happen to me personally, but it’s just more convenient to find things online now. I like the idea of print for books and magazines in theory, but it’s been years since I bought a hard-copy book rather than a Kindle version. The only ones in the last ten years or so are those that are not available in electronic format. My guess is that the WA figured most readers would shift and most new readers would be finding them online anyway, so it made sense, just like it made sense for Netflix to stop sending out the DVDs a few years ago.

No criticism here, just my two cents.

posts.name: Monica Larner
posts.cooked:
The problem with the WA online edition is that it is poorly designed and difficult to navigate. Both Vinous and Dunnuck are better.

Hi Jim,

We are currently looking at ways to improve our site and improve navigation. However, in my experience the RPWA Search capability (the Advanced Search) is unrivaled.

The powerful search tool killed any romantic nostalgia I may have felt for the print edition.

Best,
Monica

posts.name: Peter Simpson
posts.cooked:
I was a loyal TWA subscriber for over 20 years starting in 1987. However, when LPB started to run the show there, she somehow had a knack for rubbing people the wrong way. Just about every post she made was greeted with a firestorm of criticism! Monica, a fabulous communicator, tried repeatedly to pour oil on the troubled waters. Unfortunately, she wasn’t too successful, there’s not much you can do when the boss keeps putting both her feet in her mouth!

I think many subscribers quit around that time. I stuck it out until LPB started reviewing West Coast wines, and that motivated me to let my subscription lapse. Incredibly I never received any kind of notification from TWA reminding me to renew!

@William Kelley if you still have the email addresses of all those long-lost subscribers. I think an email letting people know about the change in course, and hopefully giving a brief introduction to your new West Coast reviewer, might work wonders for your circulation numbers.
